summary: Camille Fournier presents a comprehensive list of non-technical skills that distinguish senior engineers from their less experienced peers. The post argues that advancing beyond mid-level engineering requires mastering interpersonal, communication, and organizational skills that have nothing to do with writing code. These skills span running meetings effectively, mentoring others, navigating organizational politics, and influencing without authority. The list progresses from tactical skills like writing design docs to strategic abilities like convincing management to invest in technical projects.
key_points: Running meetings effectively means facilitating outcomes, not dominating the conversation
Senior engineers must learn to influence and persuade without formal authority over other teams or engineers
Emotional intelligence is critical: making others feel appreciated, not threatened, and not rolling your eyes at less technical colleagues
Career advancement requires proactively finding work and building political capital rather than waiting for assignments
Teaching and mentoring others—including helping them get promoted—becomes a core responsibility at senior levels
money_quotes: How to run a meeting, and no, being the person who talks the most in the meeting is not the same thing as running it
How to indulge a senior manager who wants to talk about technical stuff that they don't really understand, without rolling your eyes or making them feel stupid
How to get another engineer to do something for you by asking for help in a way that makes them feel appreciated
How to lead a project even though you don't manage any of the people working on the project
How to listen to other engineers' ideas without feeling threatened
How to give up your baby, that project that you built into something great, so you can do something else
How to repeat yourself enough that people start to listen
How to find interesting work on your own, instead of waiting for someone to bring it to you
key_insight: The skills that make someone a senior engineer are predominantly interpersonal and organizational, not technical—success at higher levels depends on influence, communication, and emotional intelligence.
